metadata.title: LiteracyPath Guided Reading Pack 1
metadata.description: 50 original leveled books for guided reading, K-3 progression

pages.text: Nan has a red hat.
The hat is on the mat.
Nan can not get the hat.
Sam has a plan.
Sam can tap the mat.
Nan can get the hat!
The class has a mess.
Cans and bags are on the rug.
Sam can pick up the cans.
Sam can pick up the bags.
I can help, says Nan.
The class is clean!

gr-a-03.title: The Map in the Bag
gr-a-03.theme: using a map to find a lost item

pages.text: Nan and Sam see a bag.
A map is in the bag!
The map has an X.
Nan and Sam run to the X.
The X is on a mat.
A red pen is on the mat!
Kit sees a big box.
I sit in the box, says Kit.
Dot sees the big box.
I sit in the box, says Dot.
You sit. I sit. We sit!
The box is fun for Kit and Dot!

gr-a-05.title: Nan and the Seed
gr-a-05.target_skills: short e, seed vocabulary in context

pages.text: Nan has a seed.
Nan puts the seed in a pot.
Nan gets a wet rag.
The seed is in the wet rag.
Nan lets the seed rest.
The seed is a plant!
A map shows a place.
This map shows a park.
The park has a road.
The road has a path.
The path has a pond.
A map helps you see a place!
The sun is in the sky.
It is day.
The sun goes down.
The moon is in the sky.
It is night.
Day and night go round and round!
Rain comes down from the sky.
Rain helps the plants.
The plants get a drink.
The plants grow up.
The sun helps the plants too.
Rain and sun help plants grow!
Animals need homes.
A bird has a nest in a tree.
A fox has a den in a hill.
A fish has a pond.
A bug has a log.
Each animal has a safe home!
Books are for us.
We turn the pages with care.
We keep books dry.
We put books on the shelf.
We share books with friends.
We love our books!

gr-b-06.title: Mina Fixes the Gate
gr-b-06.target_skills: short vowels, silent e preview, blends (st, nd), persistence

pages.text: Mina sees a broken gate.
The gate will not stay shut.
Mina gets a stick and some string.
She ties the stick to the gate.
The stick breaks! Mina tries again.
This time, Mina uses a plank and nails. The gate stays shut!

gr-b-07.title: The Quiet Bell
gr-b-07.target_skills: short vowels, -ell word family, community awareness

pages.text: Tess has a bell on her desk.
The bell is too quiet.
No one can hear the bell ring.
Tess gets a shell and a spoon.
She bangs the spoon on the shell.
Now the class can hear! Tess made a new bell.
Dell has a big lunch bag.
She sees Nell with no lunch.
Will you share? asks Nell.
Yes! says Dell. She splits the sandwich.
They share the chips and the drink.
Two friends, one lunch. It is the best lunch ever!

gr-b-09.title: The Kite That Waited
gr-b-09.target_skills: long i preview, short vowels, patience

pages.text: Finn has a red kite.
He runs with the kite. It will not fly.
No wind, says Finn. He sits and waits.
The trees start to sway. The wind is here!
Finn runs fast. The kite goes up, up, up!
The kite flies high in the sky. Waiting was worth it!

gr-b-10.title: Omar's Bright Idea
gr-b-10.target_skills: CVC and HFW mix, creative problem solving

pages.text: The classroom is dark and gloomy.
The bulb is burned out.
Omar looks at the sun-filled windows.
He gets foil and tape. He makes a reflector.
Omar puts the reflector by the window.
Sunlight bounces in! The room is bright. Omar saved the day!
The creek is wide. The friends need to cross.
Jade piles stones in the water. They wobble and sink.
We need a plan, says Blake. Let us use sticks.
They weave long sticks over the stones.
Mud seals the cracks. Leaves cover the top.
The bridge holds! Together, they made a safe path.

gr-c-12.title: The Lost Paintbrush
gr-c-12.target_skills: silent e, problem-solution structure, descriptive language

pages.text: Rosa needs her paintbrush for art class.
She looks in her desk. She looks in her bag.
The brush is gone! Rosa feels upset.
She sees a feather on the floor.
Rosa dips the feather in paint. It makes fine lines!
Sometimes the best tools are the ones you find.

gr-c-13.title: A Garden for Everyone
gr-c-13.target_skills: silent e, vowel teams (ea, oo), community theme

pages.text: The playground has a bare spot by the fence.
Ivy has an idea. We can plant a garden here!
Some kids bring seeds. Some bring tools.
They dig the soil. They drop in seeds.
Each day they water and weed. Green shoots peek up!
Soon the garden blooms. It is bright and beautiful and ours.

gr-c-14.title: The Rain Jar
gr-c-14.target_skills: silent e, cause and effect, environmental awareness

pages.text: The sun bakes the school garden. The leaves droop.
We need water, says Chen. But the hose is off.
Chen looks at the dark clouds. Rain is coming!
He places a big jar by the garden bed.
Plop, plop, plop! Rain fills the jar.
Chen pours the rain on the plants. Nature gave them a drink!

gr-c-15.title: The Lantern Path
gr-c-15.target_skills: silent e, vowel teams, empathy and courage

pages.text: The power goes out at school. The halls go dark.
Some kids are scared. Mei sees their worried faces.
She finds paper lanterns in the art room.
Mei lights each one with a small candle.
She places them along the hallway floor.
A warm path glows. No one is scared now. Mei led the way.
Maya and her friends build a bridge of blocks.
They test it with a toy car. Crash! The bridge falls.
"It is ruined!" stamps Leo. Maya shakes her head.
We need a base, says Maya. Wide and flat and strong.
They slide a book under the blocks. The bridge stands tall.
The toy car rolls across. Maya smiles. Never give up!

gr-d-17.title: The Honest Choice
gr-d-17.target_skills: vowel teams (oa, ow), character moral decisions

pages.text: Zoe finds a shiny coin in the lunch line.
She looks around. No one seems to notice.
Zoe clutches the coin. She thinks of the bake sale.
Then she sees a younger child crying by the door.
"Did you lose this?" Zoe asks. The child nods and beams.
Zoe feels warm inside. Doing the right thing is its own reward.

gr-d-18.title: The Smallest Seed
gr-d-18.target_skills: vowel teams (ee, ea), growth mindset metaphor
gr-d-18.theme: patience and believing in small beginnings

pages.text: In a garden of giants, one seed is tiny.
The other seeds tease it. You are too small to grow!
A gust of wind blows the tiny seed far away.
It lands in rich soil. Rain falls. Sun beams down.
A green leaf peeks out. Then two. Then ten!
The tiny seed becomes the tallest sunflower. Small starts matter.

gr-d-19.title: The Team With No Captain
gr-d-19.target_skills: r-controlled vowels, leadership and shared responsibility

pages.text: The soccer team has no captain. Everyone wants to lead.
We need one voice, says Coach Verra. Not five.
During practice, each player calls a different play.
They trip and tangle. No goal. No score. Just chaos.
Then Stan speaks up. What if we take turns leading?
They win the game. Every player scored. Together, they were the captain.

gr-d-20.title: The Window Garden
gr-d-20.target_skills: multisyllabic words, vowel teams, urban nature theme

pages.text: Ava lives in a tall building. There is no yard to tend.
She looks out the window at the brick wall.
Ava fills small pots with soil. She places them on the sill.
She plants basil, mint, and a tiny tomato vine.
Each morning she waters. Each evening she checks for sprouts.
Green leaves reach for the sky. A garden can grow anywhere.

gr-e-21.title: The Long Way Home
gr-e-21.target_skills: long vowel teams (ai, ee, oa), narrative sequencing, problem solving

pages.text: The school bus breaks down far from home.
The driver calls for help. We must wait, she says.
Eli looks at the map on his phone. There is a path through the park.
Who wants to walk? he asks. Five friends raise their hands.
They hike past ponds and meadows. They spot birds and deer.
They reach home at sunset. Sometimes the long way is the best way.

gr-e-22.title: The Debate at Table Four
gr-e-22.target_skills: complex vowel teams, persuasive language, respectful disagreement

pages.text: At lunch, table four has a big debate. Which season is best?
Winter! shouts Nia. Snow days and hot cocoa by the fire!
Spring! cries Jules. Flowers bloom and birds return to sing!
Voices rise. Faces flush. No one will change their mind.
Then Kira speaks. What if each season teaches us something?
Table four grows quiet. Maybe being right is less important than being kind.

gr-e-23.title: The Day the Lights Went Out
gr-e-23.target_skills: complex phonics, community resilience, descriptive language

pages.text: A storm knocks out the power on Maple Street.
Screens go black. Clocks blink. The fridge hums to silence.
Mr. Cruz brings flashlights. Ms. Reed shares candles from her pantry.
Neighbors gather in the street. They share food from warming grills.
Children play tag in the moonlight. Stories pass from porch to porch.
When the lights return, no one rushes inside. The dark brought them together.

gr-e-24.title: The Community Mural
gr-e-24.target_skills: complex multisyllabic words, collaborative creativity, cultural celebration

pages.text: A gray wall sits at the edge of the playground. It needs color.
Ms. Park asks each student to paint one thing they love about their town.
Diego paints the library. Amara paints the farmers market. Lena paints the river.
They paint families, pets, and trees. Each brushstroke tells a story.
The mural grows. Faces, places, memories blend into one big picture.
The whole town gathers to see. This wall belongs to everyone.

gr-e-25.title: A Promise to the Pond
gr-e-25.target_skills: complex vowels, environmental stewardship, narrative arc

pages.text: The pond behind school is full of trash. The water smells foul.
No frogs croak. No dragonflies hover. The pond is dying.
Tara makes a pledge. We will clean this pond. We will save it.
Every Friday, they pull out bottles and bags. They scoop mud.
Week by week, the water clears. Green moss returns. Tadpoles wriggle.
At last, a frog croaks. Tara keeps her promise. Nature keeps its promise too.

gr-b-31.title: How Seeds Become Plants
gr-b-31.target_skills: science vocabulary, sequence words, short vowels

pages.text: A seed is small. But it has a big job.
The seed sits in soil. It drinks up water.
A root pops out. It digs deep down.
A stem reaches up. A leaf opens wide.
The sun gives the plant food. It grows tall.
A tiny seed can become a huge plant. Nature is amazing!

gr-b-32.title: Why Bees Visit Flowers
gr-b-32.target_skills: science concepts, pollination intro, simple cause-effect

pages.text: Bees buzz from flower to flower. They are not just playing.
A flower has sweet nectar inside. The bee wants a sip.
The bee lands on a petal. Dust sticks to its legs.
That dust is pollen. It helps plants make seeds.
The bee flies to the next bloom. Pollen drops off.
Bees get food. Flowers get help. They need each other!

gr-b-33.title: What Makes Shadows?
gr-b-33.target_skills: light and shadow science, question-answer format

pages.text: Look at your shadow! It moves when you move.
A shadow needs light. It also needs something solid.
When light hits you, it stops. It cannot pass through.
The dark spot behind you is the shadow.
A big object makes a big shadow. A small object makes a small one.
Try it! Stand in the sun. Watch your shadow dance.

gr-b-34.title: How Bread Is Made
gr-b-34.target_skills: sequence and process, food science vocabulary

pages.text: Bread starts as a tiny seed. The seed is wheat.
Farmers plant wheat in fields. The sun and rain help it grow.
When wheat is dry, it turns to grain. Mills grind the grain into flour.
Bakers mix flour with water and yeast. The dough gets soft and puffy.
They bake the dough in a hot oven. The smell fills the room!
From seed to loaf, many hands help. Bread is a team effort!

gr-b-35.title: Big Machines at Work
gr-b-35.target_skills: machine vocabulary, descriptive features, action words

pages.text: Look at the big machines! Each one has a special job.
The bulldozer pushes piles of dirt. It clears the land.
The crane lifts steel beams up high. It builds towers.
The dump truck hauls rocks and sand. Its bed tips way back.
The cement mixer spins and spins. It keeps concrete smooth.
Big machines work hard. They help build our roads and homes!

gr-c-36.title: Weather Tools
gr-c-36.target_skills: science tools vocabulary, how things work, silent e

pages.text: How do we know what the weather will be? We use tools!
A thermometer measures heat. The red line rises on a hot day.
A rain gauge collects drops. It shows how much rain fell.
A wind vane points where wind blows. It spins on top of a pole.
A barometer measures air pressure. It helps predict storms.
With these tools, we can read the sky. Weather is no mystery!

gr-c-37.title: How We Use Water
gr-c-37.target_skills: everyday science, conservation awareness, descriptive language

pages.text: Water is everywhere. It is in our lakes, sinks, and bodies.
We drink water to stay healthy. It keeps our skin clear.
Farmers use water to grow food. Without it, crops would wilt.
Factories need water too. It cools hot machines.
Water helps us clean. We wash hands, clothes, and floors.
Clean water is precious. We must use it wisely and keep it pure.

gr-c-38.title: Birds Build Nests
gr-c-38.target_skills: animal behavior, process description, nature vocabulary

pages.text: Birds need safe homes for their eggs. So they build nests!
A robin weaves grass and twigs. She makes a round cup.
A hummingbird uses spider silk. The silk holds the nest to a branch.
An eagle builds a huge pile of sticks. It can weigh a ton!
A woodpecker carves a hole in a tree. No sticks needed!
Each nest is just right for its bird. Nature is a clever builder!

gr-c-39.title: From Caterpillar to Butterfly
gr-c-39.target_skills: life cycle sequence, transformation vocabulary, nature science

pages.text: A butterfly starts as an egg. The egg is no bigger than a dot!
The egg hatches. A tiny caterpillar crawls out. It is very hungry!
The caterpillar eats leaves. It grows bigger each day.
Then it spins a chrysalis. It hangs like a jewel on a stem.
Inside, big changes happen. The caterpillar turns to soup!
At last, a winged beauty breaks free. Hello, butterfly!

gr-c-40.title: Healthy Hands
gr-c-40.target_skills: health habits, procedural text, hygiene vocabulary

pages.text: Your hands touch many things. They pick up germs you cannot see.
Germs can make you sick. Washing hands keeps them away.
First, wet your hands under warm water.
Next, add soap. Rub your palms, backs, and between fingers.
Scrub for twenty seconds. Sing a song to keep time!
Rinse and dry. Now your hands are clean. Good job!
Bridges carry people and cars across rivers and valleys. How do they stay up?
A beam bridge is flat and simple. The beam pushes down on two strong posts called piers.
An arch bridge curves up. The curve spreads weight to both ends.
A suspension bridge hangs from cables. Towers hold the cables tight.
The key is balance. Every part shares the load. Engineers test each bridge.
Next time you cross a bridge, look at its shape. Smart design keeps you safe!

gr-d-42.title: Why Leaves Change
gr-d-42.target_skills: seasonal science, chlorophyll concept, nature cycles
gr-d-42.theme: the science of autumn leaf color

pages.text: In summer, leaves are green. But hidden colors wait inside.
A green chemical called chlorophyll helps leaves catch sunlight.
As days get shorter, trees make less chlorophyll. The green fades away.
Then yellow and orange shine through. These colors were there all along!
Some trees make red and purple too. Sugar trapped in leaves makes these hues.
When leaves fall, they feed the soil. New green leaves will come in spring!
One out of every three bites of food exists because of pollinators.
Bees are the most famous pollinators. But butterflies, birds, and bats help too.
When a bee visits a bloom, pollen dusts its fuzzy body.
The bee flies to the next flower. Pollen rubs off. The flower can now make fruit.
Without pollinators, apples, berries, and almonds would vanish.
Planting flowers helps pollinators thrive. They feed us. We should feed them too.

gr-d-44.title: How Communities Share Spaces
gr-d-44.theme: shared community places and their purposes

pages.text: A community is a group of people living in the same area. They share many spaces.
A park gives people a place to play and rest. It has grass, trees, and paths.
A library holds books for everyone. You can borrow stories and learn new facts.
A market lets people buy fresh food. Farmers sell fruits and vegetables there.
A school is where children learn. Teachers help them read, write, and think.
These spaces bring people together. When we share, our community grows stronger.

gr-d-45.title: What Soil Is Made Of
gr-d-45.target_skills: earth science, layers and composition, scientific vocabulary

pages.text: Look down at the ground beneath your feet. That brown matter is soil.
Soil is a mix of many things. It has rock bits, water, air, and living matter.
Tiny rocks in soil come from big rocks that broke apart over time.
Dead leaves and plants rot in the soil. They add nutrients that help new plants grow.
Worms and bugs live in soil. They dig tunnels that let air and water move through.
Without soil, most plants could not grow. Soil keeps our world green and fed.

gr-e-46.title: How Water Changes the Land
gr-e-46.target_skills: earth science, erosion and weathering, process description
gr-e-46.theme: water as a force that shapes landscapes

pages.text: Water is patient and powerful. Over time, it changes the shape of the land.
Rain falls on mountains. Tiny drops find cracks in the rock and freeze.
Frozen water expands. The cracks grow wider. Bits of rock break off.
Rivers carry sand and stone. They grind against canyon walls, carving deep paths.
Waves hit the shore again and again. They smooth rocks and shape beaches.
Every river valley, every canyon, every beach tells a water story.

gr-e-47.title: Planning a Safe Route
gr-e-47.target_skills: spatial reasoning, safety concepts, procedural planning
gr-e-47.theme: how to plan safe walking routes

pages.text: Getting from one place to another takes planning. A safe route keeps you out of danger.
Start by looking at a map. Find your home and your goal. Trace the streets between them.
Pick paths with sidewalks. Walking on the street is risky. Sidewolds separate you from cars.
Choose well-lit streets. Darkness hides tripe and other hazards. Light helps you see.
Cross at corners and use crosswalks. Drivers expect walkers there. Always look both ways.
A good route is safe, short, and simple. Plan ahead, walk smart, and stay alert.

gr-e-48.title: Why Animals Migrate
gr-e-48.target_skills: animal behavior, seasonal adaptation, ecosystem connections
gr-e-48.theme: the reasons and methods of animal migration

pages.text: Every year, billions of animals travel long distances. This journey is called migration.
Most animals migrate to find food. When winter comes, insects and plants disappear.
Birds fly south to warm places. They follow the same paths their parents used.
Some whales swim to cold waters to feed. There, tiny sea creatures bloom in summer.
Monarch butterflies travel thousands of miles. It takes four generations to complete the round trip!
Migrating animals do not use maps. They use the sun, stars, and Earths magnetic field to guide them.

gr-e-49.title: How Simple Machines Help
gr-e-49.target_skills: physics basics, six simple machines, real-world applications

pages.text: A simple machine is a tool that makes work easier. There are six kinds.
A lever helps you lift heavy things. A seesaw on the playground is a lever.
A wheel and axle let things roll. Bikes, cars, and scooters all use this pair.
A pulley uses a rope and wheel to lift loads up high. Flagpoles use pulleys.
An inclined plane is a slanted surface. Ramps help wheelchairs roll up stairs.
Simple machines are everywhere. They make our daily tasks possible.

gr-e-50.title: Reading Like a Researcher
gr-e-50.target_skills: research habits, active reading, literacy metacognition
gr-e-50.theme: how to read and learn like a researcher

pages.text: Researchers do not just read words. They ask questions, make connections, and dig deeper.
Before reading, look at the title and pictures. What do you already know? What do you hope to learn?
While reading, pause to think. Does this make sense? Highlight important facts.
After reading, summarize what you learned. Say it in your own words.
Good readers check sources. Is the author an expert? Is the information current?
Reading is not just looking at words. It is thinking, wondering, and growing your mind.
